questions.text: The Lion Capital at Sarnath, adopted as India's national emblem, was built by which ruler?
questions.explanation: The Sarnath Lion Capital (Ashoka Stambha) was built by Emperor Ashoka (~250 BCE) at Sarnath, where Buddha gave his first sermon. It shows four lions back to back atop a circular abacus. The Government of India adopted it as the national emblem in 1950. The Ashoka Chakra from this capital appears on the national flag.

questions.text: Who deciphered the Brahmi script in which Ashoka's edicts are written?
questions.explanation: James Prinsep, Secretary of the Asiatic Society of Bengal, deciphered the Brahmi script in 1837. This breakthrough allowed scholars to read Ashoka's rock edicts and pillar inscriptions for the first time in centuries. He also deciphered Kharosthi script.

questions.text: The Kalinga War fought by Ashoka is mentioned in which edict?
questions.explanation: Rock Edict XIII describes Ashoka's horror at the Kalinga War (261 BCE) — reporting 150,000 deportees, 100,000 killed, and many times that dying. It describes his conversion to Buddhism and his rejection of military conquest in favour of Dhamma-vijaya (conquest by righteousness).

options: State administration, economics, and military strategy
questions.explanation: The Arthashastra by Kautilya (Chanakya/Vishnugupta) is a treatise on statecraft, economic policy, military strategy, and political organisation. It deals with the Saptanga (seven elements of a state), taxation, espionage, diplomacy, and the duties of a king.

questions.text: What did Chandragupta Maurya receive from Seleucus Nicator in exchange for 500 war elephants?
options: Vast territories including Arachosia, Gedrosia, Paropanisadae, and Aria
questions.explanation: After defeating Seleucus Nicator (~305 BCE), Chandragupta received vast territories in the northwest (modern Afghanistan/Pakistan: Arachosia, Gedrosia, Paropanisadae, Aria) in exchange for 500 war elephants. This also led to the Treaty of Friendship and a matrimonial alliance (Seleucus's daughter married into the Maurya family, or Chandragupta married Seleucus's daughter).

options: A code of social and moral conduct
A specific religious doctrine of Buddhism
Based on principles like non-violence, truthfulness, and respect for all
Propagated through rock and pillar edicts
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Dhamma was NOT Buddhism per se — it was a syncretic moral code emphasising non-violence (ahimsa), truthfulness, respect for elders, tolerance of all religions, and charity. While Ashoka was personally a Buddhist, his Dhamma was meant for ALL subjects regardless of religion. He never imposed Buddhism.

questions.text: Chandragupta Maurya ended his life by:
options: Dying in battle against the Seleucids
Dying of old age in Pataliputra
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya converted to Jainism in his later years and abdicated in favour of his son Bindusara. He went to Shravanabelagola (Karnataka) with the Jain monk Bhadrabahu and fasted to death (Sallekhana/Santhara — voluntary death by fasting, a Jain practice). This is well-attested in Jain tradition.

questions.text: Bindusara (Ashoka's father) was known by what Greek name?
questions.explanation: Bindusara was known to the Greeks as 'Amitrochates' (from Sanskrit Amitraghata = 'destroyer of enemies'). He corresponded with the Seleucid king Antiochus I. 'Sandrokottos' is the Greek name for Chandragupta Maurya. 'Piodasses' is Ashoka's name in some Greek/Aramaic inscriptions.

questions.text: The Mauryan provincial administration used officials called 'Kumaras'. What were they?
options: Princes of the royal family appointed as governors of major provinces
Tax collectors appointed by local village councils
Judicial officers at the district level
questions.explanation: Kumaras (princes) were governors of the four major provinces (Uttarapatha/Taxila, Dakshinapatha/Suvarnagiri, Avantiratha/Ujjain, Prachya/Tosali). This kept strategic provinces under direct royal family control. Below provinces were districts (Janapadas) and villages (Grama).

questions.text: Ashoka sent his son Mahendra (Mahinda) to spread Buddhism to which region?
questions.explanation: Ashoka sent his son Mahendra (Mahinda) and daughter Sanghamitra to Sri Lanka during the Third Buddhist Council (~250 BCE). Mahinda converted the Sri Lankan king Devanampiya Tissa to Buddhism. Sanghamitra brought a cutting of the Bodhi Tree to Sri Lanka. Sri Lanka has been predominantly Buddhist ever since.

questions.text: The Pataliputra City Council mentioned by Megasthenes had how many members?
questions.explanation: According to Megasthenes' Indica, the Pataliputra municipal committee had 30 members divided into 6 committees of 5 each, responsible for: industries and handicrafts; visitors/foreigners; births and deaths registration; trade and weights; public sales; and manufactured goods tax. This is a precise figure directly tested in SSC.

questions.text: The Maurya Empire collapsed when Brihadratha, the last Maurya king, was killed by:
questions.explanation: Pushyamitra Shunga, the Senapati (commander-in-chief) under Brihadratha, killed the last Maurya king while reviewing troops in 185 BCE and founded the Shunga dynasty. This marked the end of the Maurya Empire — which had lasted 137 years (322–185 BCE).

questions.text: Which Maurya ruler is also known as 'Devanampiya' and 'Piyadassi' in his own edicts?
questions.explanation: Ashoka uses the epithets 'Devanampiya' (Beloved of the Gods) and 'Piyadassi' (of Pleasant Appearance) in his own rock and pillar edicts. His name 'Ashoka' was not mentioned in the edicts themselves — identification was made through cross-referencing Sri Lankan chronicles (Dipavamsa, Mahavamsa).

questions.text: What is the 'Saptanga' theory mentioned in the Arthashastra?
options: Seven ethical principles for personal conduct
Seven elements of a state: king, ministers, territory, fortified capital, treasury, army, and allies
Seven types of taxes in the Mauryan revenue system
Seven musical notes of Indian classical music
questions.explanation: The Saptanga (seven-limbed) theory in Kautilya's Arthashastra describes the seven essential elements of a state: Swami (king), Amatya (ministers), Janapada (territory with population), Durga (fortified capital), Kosha (treasury), Danda (army), and Mitra (allies). This is the foundation of Kautilya's political theory.

questions.text: Ashoka's 'Major Rock Edicts' are inscribed in which language/script?
options: Pali in Brahmi script (also Kharosthi in NW India, Greek/Aramaic in Afghanistan)
questions.explanation: Ashoka's edicts are primarily in Prakrit (Magadhi dialect) written in Brahmi script. In the northwest (Punjab, Afghanistan), Kharosthi script and Greek/Aramaic languages were also used. The Shahbazgarhi and Mansehra edicts (now Pakistan) use Kharosthi.

questions.text: The Mandala theory in Arthashastra states that:
options: The king's territory should be shaped like a circle
Your neighbour is your natural enemy and your neighbour's neighbour is your natural friend
All states must form alliances based on shared religion
Trade with distant kingdoms is more profitable than with neighbours
questions.explanation: Kautilya's Mandala theory holds that in the circle of states, your neighbour is your natural enemy (aritrastra) because you share a border and compete for resources, while your neighbour's neighbour is your natural ally (mitra) because you share a common enemy. This is pure realpolitik.
